coredata.dc:title: Nanosilver-doped carbon quantum dots for industrialization of efficacious textiles with superior UV-resistance and antimicrobial performance
coredata.dc:creator: Alzahrani, Seraj Omar
Al-Ghamdi, S.A.
Alsahag, Mansoor
Alatawi, Kahdr
Alaysuy, Omaymah
Al-Ahmed, Zehbah A.
El-Metwaly, Nashwa M.
coredata.dc:description: Numerous researches were interestingly considered with investigation of unique strategies for industrialization of efficacious textiles to find wide-scaled applicability in different purposes. The demonstrated approach represents unique strategy for manufacturing of fluorescent/UV-resistant/microbicide cotton via exploitation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and silver nanoparticles-doped carbon quantum dots (AgNPs-CQDs) to be applicable as efficacious textiles. Whereas, both CQDs and AgNPs were formerly clustered from alginate biopolymer. Topographical features and particle size of AgNPs (7.5 ± 2.6 nm), CQDs (4.9 ± 1.7 nm) & AgNPs-CQDs (4.0 ± 1.4 nm) were investigated via microscopic images and Zetasizer data. Spectral mapping data for the photoluminescent emission showed that, AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs-CQDs were exhibited with two intense peaks at 370 nm & 490 nm. Cotton fabrics were sequentially treated with CQDs, Ag-CQDs & AgNPs-CQDs to prepare CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton, respectively. Thermal stability, colorimetric properties/color strength, UV-resistance and antimicrobial potentiality were studied for all the prepared samples. AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton exhibited excellent UV-resistance (UPF, 43.4) and antimicrobial activity, whereas the diameter of inhibition zone was estimated for its affinity against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans, to be 14 mm, 18 mm and 16 mm, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
CQDs@Cotton
Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton
UV-resistance
Antimicrobial performance
Thermal stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism for preparation of the efficacious textiles
Characterization of CQDs, AgNPs & AgNPs doped-CQDs
Characterization of the prepared efficacious textiles

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Deanship of Scientific Research at Umm Al-Qura University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Deanship of Scientific Research at Umm Al-Qura University for supporting this work by Grant Code: ( 22UQU4350527DSR17 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and size distribution for the supernatant of synthesized samples; [a] AgNPs, [b] CQDs, [c] Ag-CQDs and [d] AgNPs-CQDs. [e] Particle size from zetaszier.
Scanning microscopic images for the functionalized cotton fabric; [a] cotton, [b] CQDs@Cotton, [c] Ag-CQDs@Cotton and [d] AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton.
[a] IR spectra for the functionalized cotton fabric and [b] XRD for the functionalized cotton fabric.
Color parameters for the functionalized cotton fabrics.
Transmission and UV-protection data for the functionalized cotton fabrics.
Antimicrobial results (inhibition zone) for the functionalized cotton fabrics.

head.ce:title: Nanosilver-doped carbon quantum dots for industrialization of efficacious textiles with superior UV-resistance and antimicrobial performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Numerous researches were interestingly considered with investigation of unique strategies for industrialization of efficacious textiles to find wide-scaled applicability in different purposes. The demonstrated approach represents unique strategy for manufacturing of fluorescent/UV-resistant/microbicide cotton via exploitation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and silver nanoparticles-doped carbon quantum dots (AgNPs-CQDs) to be applicable as efficacious textiles. Whereas, both CQDs and AgNPs were formerly clustered from alginate biopolymer. Topographical features and particle size of AgNPs (7.5 ± 2.6 nm), CQDs (4.9 ± 1.7 nm) & AgNPs-CQDs (4.0 ± 1.4 nm) were investigated via microscopic images and Zetasizer data. Spectral mapping data for the photoluminescent emission showed that, AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs-CQDs were exhibited with two intense peaks at 370 nm & 490 nm. Cotton fabrics were sequentially treated with CQDs, Ag-CQDs & AgNPs-CQDs to prepare CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton, respectively. Thermal stability, colorimetric properties/color strength, UV-resistance and antimicrobial potentiality were studied for all the prepared samples. AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton exhibited excellent UV-resistance (UPF, 43.4) and antimicrobial activity, whereas the diameter of inhibition zone was estimated for its affinity against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans, to be 14 mm, 18 mm and 16 mm, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant data are within the manuscript and available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) were identified as unique family of optically active nanostructures as it was shown to be advantageous with biocompatibility and nontoxicity, that make it to be appropriate as alternative for metallic quantum dots and organic colorants in various fields such as electronic, sensing, and biomedical purposes [1–5]. While, CQDs exhibit superior characteristics, like, high photo-bleaching resistance, cost effectiveness and simple synthetic techniques and easy for bio-conjugation [6–11]. Heteroatom containing carbon compounds were reported to be successively exploited as CQDs precursors for synthesis of CQDs, whereas, it was approved that, the photoluminescence of CQDs is mainly dependent on the chemical composition of the original precursor [12–16]. Ren et al., reported that, using different ratios of sodium citrate and urea of as molecular precursors resulted in generation of CQDs with different emission spectral maps [15]. Variability in emission maps was owing to the different contents of heteroatom rich surface decorative groups. In accordance with our knowledge, synthesis of CQDs using alginate as molecular precursor was not previously reported in literature, while, it is advantageous with cheapness, novelty, simplicity and could be wide-scaled applied for successive nucleation of CQDs.

Doping of inorganic metals within CQDs was reported to superiorly advancing their functionality and optical activity [17]. Doping of ZnO or ZnS within CQDs was performed via alkaline hydrolysis or precipitation with sodium sulfide in aqueous dispersion of the nanoparticles with zinc acetate, respectively. Whereas, the prepared colloids of ZnO-CQDs and ZnS-CQDs exhibited strong bluish green photoluminescence emission, while, the mechanism was hypothesized to be attributed to surface passivation akin to organic passivation agent [17]. Sun et al., also prepared green photoluminescent CQDs by doping zinc sulfide and titanium dioxide [18]. Recently, zinc-doped CQDs with strong yellow photoluminescence was successfully prepared using zinc ions and citric acid as the molecular precursor via a one-pot solvothermal technique, as reported by Cheng [19]. Moreover, manganese doped and rubidium doped CQDs with multi-color emission were also reported [20–22].

Advancement in the textile industry and technology leading to increment in the demand of the multi-functional textile products, in addition to the world wide-scaled requirement of multi-efficacious textiles to be applicable in different purposes, such as, medical and healthcare, sporting, UV-protection, camping military and fitness textiles [23,24]. The comfortability of the produced functional garments is ascribed as a vital requirement during its manufacturing. Recently, numerous reports were considered with the modification of textile materials with multi-functions such as self-cleaning [25–27], flame retardant & water repellent [26], antimicrobial [28,29] anti-insects [30,31] and UV-protective performance [28,32–34]. The innovative studying for functionalization of textile materials as masks, air-filtration matrix for protection against toxic gases is mainly dependent on the highly adsorption action of the hydrophobic activated carbonic structures, while, the adsorption of such type of materials is not in conflict with acting as a secondary emitter with their pollution [35].

The demonstrated approach represents unique strategy for manufacturing of fluorescent/UV-resistant/microbicide cotton via exploitation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and silver nanoparticles-doped carbon quantum dots (AgNPs-CQDs) to be applicable as efficacious textiles. Whereas, both CQDs and AgNPs were formerly clustered from alginate biopolymer. Successive ingraining of CQDs &AgNPs was confirmed via TEM and Zetasizer analysis. The synthesized nanosilver doped-CQDs were sequentially exploited in production of antimicrobial/UV-resistant cotton fabrics. Cotton fabrics were sequentially treated with CQDs, Ag-CQDs & AgNPs-CQDs to prepare CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton, respectively. Thermal stability, colorimetric properties/color strength, UV-resistance & antimicrobial potentiality were systematically studied for all the prepared samples. The functionality of treated fabrics was investigated via FT-IR, SEM, EDX, XRD, photoluminescence, microbicide potentiality and UV-resistance performance. Moreover, their colorimetric properties and thermal stability were studied for all the modified fabrics.

Sodium alginate (91–106 %, Loba Chemie, Mumbai–India), Silver nitrate (99.5 %, from Panreac, Barcelona – Spain) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Sigma-Aldrich) were applied without any further purifying. Scoured and bleached plain-woven 100 % cotton fabrics (185 g/m2) supplied by Misr Company for Spinning and Weaving, El-Mehalla El-Kobra – Egypt.

For synthesis of AgNPs; 1 mM of silver nitrate was drop wisely added to 1 g/L of sodium alginate to be alkali solubilized with 10 g/L NaOH, under magnetic stirring for 30 min at 70 °C. CQDs were uniquely ingrained from sodium alginate as follows; 2 g/L of sodium alginate was alkali hydrolyzed with 20 g/L of sodium hydroxide and subsequently the reaction liquor was left under magnetic stirring at 90 °C for 30 min. Under hydrothermal conditions, for complete clustering of CQDs, the produced colloid was poured in a vertical autoclave reactor to be transferred in muffle at 160 °C for 5 h. Lastly, the reaction liquor with dark yellowish color was cooled down in atmospheric air for sequential overnight dialysis with distilled water using pur-A-lyzer dialysis kit (MWCO 6–8 kDa from Sigma-Aldrich). On the other hand, Ag-CQDs and AgNPs-CQDs were prepared by addition of Ag+ and AgNPs, respectively, to alkali hydrolyzed alginate before exposing to hydrothermal conditions.

CQDs, Ag-CQDs and AgNPs-CQDs were uploaded onto the cotton fabrics by dip coating process. In 50 mL of the formerly prepared colloids, pieces of cotton fabrics (0.5 g) were impregnated in the prepared colloids separately for 20 min with continuous stirring. Afterwards, fabrics pieces were left for drying at 70 °C before characterization. The prepared fabrics were labeled as CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton.

For Characterization of CQDs, AgNPs & AgNPs-doped CQDs, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope from Japan JEOL-JEM-1200″ was applied for determination of topographical features and size average, as it was estimated by “4 pi analysis software from USA” for at least 50 particles. Absorption spectra for AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs were manifested at 200–700 nm via Cary 100 UV-VIS, UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer, from Agilent. Mean particle size of the prepared AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs was detected via “Zetasizer analyzer” (Malvern Nano ZS, from Malvern Instruments Ltd. – UK) with dynamic light scattering technique at 25 °C in insulated chamber. The optical activity for the prepared AgNPs, CQDs and AgNPs doped-CQDs in ultraviolet-visible range was followed up via spectro-florometer (ASCO FP8300). The results were collected at room temperature with exciting at 340 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) analyses were performed using K-ALPHA from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA) with the monochromatic of X-Ray Al K-alpha radiation.

The prepared fabrics (CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton) were characterized via high resolution scanning electron microscope (HRSEM Quanta FEG 250 with field emission gun, FEI Company – Netherlands). The elemental analysis was also determined via energy dispersive X-ray analyzer (EDAX AME-TEK analyzer). Infrared spectral mapping for CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton were collected using “Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer”. The absorbance spectral data were ranged in 4000–400 cm−1 (4 cm−1 resolution and 64 scanning times with rate of 2 mm/s). Thermal stability of CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton was approved via performing the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) STD Q600 V20.9 Build 20. TGA decomposition was estimated at 600 °C, using heating rate of 10 °C/ min. Photoluminescence spectral data were also obtained by Jasco FP-6500 spectro-fluorometer (150 W Xenon lamp, with concave holographic grating from Japan (1800 grooves/mm emission monochromator and coupled with a second photomultiplier). Whereas the samples were exposed for excitation at 360 nm and the emission spectral data were sequentially collected at room temperature. Color coordinates (L, a*, b*) of CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton were detected using colorimeter (UltraScan Pro, Hunter Lab, USA), pulsed xenon lamps as light source, 10° observer (with D65 illuminant, d/2 viewing geometry and measurement area of 2 mm). Color strength (K/S) was estimated at wavelength of 535 nm and absorption was detected at 350–650 nm. All of the obtained data were estimated for three times while, the mean value was considered. The effect of successive uploading of the prepared nanostructures on the mechanical properties of the modified fabrics were studied, whereas, tensile strength (MPa) and elongation at break (%) for native cotton and the modified fabrics were estimated according to ASTM method D2256–66T using the strip test (Asano machine MFG. Co. Ltd., Japan). The silver within cotton was extracted by using 10 % nitric acid at 80 °C for 1 h. The extracted silver was estimated by using the 240FS AA flame atomic absorption spectrophotometer (Agilent 200 series – USA).

The biocidal performance of CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton against different microbial strains was approved via the qualitative method of “Kirby-Bauer disk diffusion technique” [36,37]. In this process, all the prepared “CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton were tested for their antimicrobial potency against two pathogens of –ve gram bacterial species “Escherichia coli”, +ve gram bacterial species “staphylococcus Aureus” and fungal strain “Candida albicans”. In the inhibition zone technique “disk diffusion method”, the tested microbial species were existed in the media for preparation of microbial colloids.100 μL of microbial dispersion was suspended in agar plates corresponding to the broth in which it was maintained. 10 μL of the examined samples were diffused in the middle of the prepared plate to be incubated at 37 °C for one day. The diameters of the inhibition zones were estimated in mL via slipping calipers referring to NCCLS, 1997 [38]. After incubation at 37 °C for one day, the colony forming units (CFU) were counted for each dilution [38,39].

T% (Transmission %) for UV radiation (UVR) over CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton were detected via JASCO V-750 spectrophotometer (Japan) in the range of 280–400 nm (with two nanometer intervals). Ultraviolet protection factor (UPF), resistance in UV-A (315–400) region (UVA) and in UV–B (280–315) region (UVB) were detected via AATCC test 183–2010.44. For each sample, the detection was carried out for three different times, and the average was concerned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for preparation of the efficacious textiles
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, sodium alginate as a biocompatible/nontoxic/abundant polymeric block was exploited for clustering CQDs, AgNPs & AgNPs doped-CQDs. For synthesis of AgNPs, alginate macromolecules were alkali hydrolyzed to increase its accessibility to act the dual role of reducer and stabilizer to produce the required NPs. However, the mechanism of CQDs clustering could be hypothesized as follows; under the hydrothermal conditions, the alkali hydrolyzed alginate macromolecules are supposed to be fragmented, de-polymerized, re-polymerized, oxidized and eventually aromatized, to give size and shape controllable CQDs as aromatic graphite sheets, decorated with oxygen containing functional groups (OH & CO) [40–42]. In case of AgNPs doped-CQDs, AgNPs were nucleated separately to be sequentially added to alkali hydrolyzed alginate before exposing to hydrothermal conditions. For preparation of the functionalized cotton fabric, the synthesized nanostructures were loaded upon the cotton fabrics. Whereas AgNPs were coordinately bonded to the functional groups of fabric backbone. However, via hydrogen bonding, the surface decorative groups for CQDs or AgNPs doped-CQDs, were suggested to be chemically bonded with hydroxyl functional groups in cotton matrix [26,43,44]. Additionally, all of the prepared nanostructures might be physically entrapped within the intermolecular spaces of the fabric matrix [28,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, AgNPs & AgNPs doped-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Geometrical and topographical features of the prepared CQDs (quantum yield 11.6 %), AgNPs & AgNPs doped-CQDs (quantum yield 13.2 %) could be obviously shown via the presented microscopic images of Transmission Electron Microscope in Fig. 1 , moreover, the estimated size distribution for each was also demonstrated. From the plotted data, sodium alginate was successfully exploited in nucleation of AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs with controllable size and geometry, with size distribution of 7.5 ± 2.6 nm (Fig. 2a), 4.9 ± 1.7 nm (Fig. 2b) & 4.0 ± 1.4 nm (Fig. 2d), respectively. For more confirmation, the as-prepared nano-colloids were also analyzed via zetasizer for estimation of particle size to be presented in Fig. 2e, whereas AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs were exhibited with particle size of 11.7 ± 1.2 nm, 7.5 ± 1.1 nm & 18.2 ± 1.5 nm, respectively. So, it could be depicted that, the investigated technique is compatible for tailoring alkali hydrolyzed alginate in successive nucleation of size and shape regulated AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs. As it is well known that, the smaller is the size of the nanostructure, the wider is surface area, the prepared AgNPs with controllable size and regulated shape were acted as a supporting template for nucleation of smaller sized hybrid nanostructure of AgNPs-CQDs compared to CQDs [45,46]/.

UV–Vis. spectroscopic maps for regulated AgNPs, CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs that were nucleated from alginate biopolymer were presented in Fig. S1. From the spectral data, it could be declared that, the characteristic SPR band of AgNPs was obviously detected at 440 nm, whereas, CQDs were shown with sharp peaks at 210 nm & 298 nm that are characterized for π-π* transition (conjugation in aromatic clusters) and n-π* transition (electron radiative relaxing in a broad shoulder with long extension in visible range, for decorative groups as oxygen containing surficial groups, OH & CO), respectively [47,48]. The optical characters of the as-nucleated CQDs were reported as it is a reflect of the surface decoration for CQDs with the heteroatom containing functional groups [26,43,44]. Moreover, the as-mentioned characteristic bands of both CQDs & AgNPs were observably detected for AgNPs doped-CQDs.

Photoluminescence was investigated via fluorescent emission for all of the prepared nano-colloids that were currently prepared from alginate biopolymer, whereas, Fig. S1 represents the spectrum of emission (excitation at 375 nm) and from the represented data, it could be observably shown that, the emission bands in UV–Visible range can affirm the composition of CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs with non-bonding and mobile pi- electrons for the decorated groups as heteroatoms containing groups (OH & CO) [47,48]. Fig. S2 represents the infrared spectral data for CQDs, Ag-CQDs & AgNPs-CQDs, to show that, addition of either Ag or AgNPs did not show any effects on the spectrum of CQDs, meaning that, there is no change in the chemical composition of CQDs. Additionally, Fig. S3 views the estimated results of XPS to show that, the prepared CQDs are characterized with oxygen containing decorative groups; hydroxyl and carbonyl groups.

The surface decoration of CQDs is mainly affected on the location of absorption bands, that sequentially results in the coloration of CQDs containing colloids for endowing their feasible colorimetric detection. Additionally, the excitation at 375 nm leaded to detection of significant sharp emission band in the visible range (480 nm, green region). Photoluminescence of CQDs as approved from the above-illustrated results can clarify that, the clustered CQDs specialized with significant optical characters in visible radiation. However, addition of Ag or AgNPs resulted in quenching for fluorescence of CQDs which might be due to the interaction between metal and decorative group in CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared efficacious textiles
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton (silver content 54 mg/g) and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton (AgNPs content 77 mg/g) as the desirably required efficacious textiles, were prepared via successive loading of CQDs, Ag-CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs upon cotton fabrics. Whereas, their topographical features were investigated via SEM images, as, the microscopic images, EDX signals and elemental mapping were plotted in Fig. 2. SEM microscopic images showed that the particles of the uploaded nanostructures were obviously seen on the surface of the prepared fabrics. Additionally, EDX data showed that, the characteristic peaks of C, O & Ag were observed in case of Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton. These affirmed the successive uploading of the formerly nucleated nanostructures (CQDs, Ag-CQDs, AgNPs doped-CQDs) within the fabric matrix.

Cotton, CQDs@cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton were characterized via FTIR as represented in Fig. 3a. The spectral data confirmed that, all fabrics were characterized by absorption peaks referred to their cellulosic backbone at 3320–3263 cm−1, 2870 cm−1, 1643 cm−1, 1420–1334 cm−1 and 1014 cm−1, that are characteristic for OH, CH aliphatic, CO, H-C-H and asymmetric C-O-C, respectively [49,50]. After successive uploading with CQDs or Ag-CQDs or AgNPs-CQDs, all of the referred absorbance bands were retained, besides, the characteristic peak of CC aromatic ring that was detected at 1574 cm−1. Additionally, by comparing with the spectrum of native cotton, the bands referring to CO, H-C-H & asymmetric C-O-C were observed to be more in intense after loading of either CQDs or Ag-CQDs or AgNPs-CQDs upon the fabrics, to confirm the successive bonding and immobilization of the prepared nanostructures within cotton matrix.

Fig. 3b represented XRD analysis for CQDs@cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton, and the presented results showed that, CQDs@cotton was detected with three characteristic bands at 2θ = 15.1°, 17.8° & 23.7° referring to CQDs [51]. While, AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton were shown with additional specialized peaks assigned for silver nanoparticles at 2θ° = 35.1°, 38.4° and 44.6°, typical for (111) and (200) of face center crystalline (FCC) structure (JCPDS data number 04-0783 card and 4-0784 card) [52,53]. These could approve that, uploading of the prepared nanostructures did not affect the crystallinity degree of the cellulosic backbone for the modified fabrics.

Manufacturing of thermally stable efficacious textiles via successive uploading of the as-prepared nano-colloids could be ascribed as a new challenge for researchers considered with the field of textile technology and industry. According to our knowledge, no research reports were investigated with the exploitation of metal nanoparticles doped-CQDs in functionalization of thermally stable textile materials. Fig. S4 showed the results of thermal gravimetrical analysis for CQDs@cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton, to show the effect of immobilization for the prepared nanostructures in enhancing the thermal stability of the treated fabrics. TGA (thermo-gravimetrical analysis) is a key parameter of thermal stabilization, whereas the changing of fabric weight is evaluated with temperature elevation by time.

From the demonstrated data, TG percent was estimated to be around 80 % even at highly elevated temperature of near 300 °C in case of CQDs@cotton and retained to be 100 % full thermally stable even at 300 °C for AgNPs-CQDs@cotton. However, two stages of weight loss were estimated, as it can be clearly observed that, weight loss percentage in both stages was notably lower for AgNPs-CQDs@cotton which confirmed the effect of nano‑silver doping for improving the thermal stability of the treated fabrics, meanwhile, complete domination of AgNPs doped-CQDs affected in higher thermal stability for the treated fabric in the studied temperature range [54,55]. Moreover, according to the estimated data in Table S1, it could be declared that, compared to the evaluated data of tensile strength and elongation percent for native cotton and the modified fabrics, the successive immobilization of the prepared nanostructures did not show significant effects on the mechanical properties of the tested fabrics.

To affirm the effect of AgNPs doping on the color changing of the prepared fabrics; color strength (K/S) and color coordinates (L*, a* and b*) were estimated and the results were represented in Table 1 & Fig. S5. From the tabulated data, it could be described that; metal nanoparticles incorporation into the prepared fabrics resulted in significant decrement for L* value, attributing to the successive coordination and stabilization of metal nanoparticles [27,29,56,57]. Compared to native cotton (86.2), loading of the prepared nanostructures upon the cotton fabric resulted in decrement of lightness index (L*) to be 79.71 & 40.24 for CQDs@cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@cotton, respectively. Additionally, whiteness index (WI) was also estimated to be 75.29 for native cotton, whereas it was evaluated to be significantly diminished to 6.98 for CQDs@cotton, however, it was greatly lowered to for cotton fabric uploaded with AgNPs doped CQDs (AgNPs-CQDs@cotton) to be −31.67.

Moreover, Fig. S5 also showed the effect of immobilizing the as-prepared nanostructures on the color strength (K/S) of the treated fabrics to show that, treatment of cotton fabric with nano-colloid prepared with doping of AgNPs within CQDs resulted in production of cotton fabric with significantly highest K/S value of 4.89 compared to native cotton (0.18) and CQDs@cotton (0.5). All of the estimated data is in accordance with the previously illustrated results.

Cotton fabrics were found to be one of the widely applicable textiles in different purposes, owing to their biocompatibility, comfortability, and breathability, but it exhibits some of disadvantages like its high flammability, low ultraviolet blocking, and biological attacking [58–60]. Moreover, protective textiles should be specialized with high UV-light reflectance and/or absorptivity so as it can prevent UV- radiation from threatening the body. Therefore, recent approaches were demonstrated different techniques for manufacturing UV-resistant textiles, whereas, numerous systematic reports were studied for exploitation of metal oxides (like, titanium dioxide, silicon dioxide, zirconium dioxide, magnesium dioxide and zinc oxide), and organic polymers were successively applied for chemical modification of cotton to acquire textiles special characters [61–63]. In the current approach, an innovative strategy was presented for acquirement the prepared fabric UV-resistance performance via successive immobilization of CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs. Transmission percentage via both of AS/NZ & AATCC standard methods and Ultraviolet Protection Factor were estimated for all the prepared fabrics as they could be identified as key parameters for evaluation of the ultraviolet resistance performance. According to the collected data in Table 2 , so as it could reveal that, the blocking percentage of the prepared samples. Ultraviolet resistance performance was analyzed in a wavelength range of 280–400 nm, as shown in Fig. S6, for all the prepared fabrics. In accordance with AATCC standard method, T% of cotton fabric was detected to be ca. 63.5 % and 70.4 % for both of UV-A and UV-B, respectively. According to the demonstrated results, compared to cotton uploaded with CQDs (CQDs-cotton, UPF 11.7 ± 0.7, UVA T% 18.1 % & UVB T% 6.0 %), T% was observably lowered after immobilization of CQDs doped with AgNPs (AgNPs-CQDs cotton, UPF 43.4 ± 1.1, UVA T% 2.3 % & UVB T% 1.3 %). Meanwhile, doping of AgNPs within CQDs sheets to be successively uploaded within cotton resulted in rating the produced AgNPs-CQDs cotton with excellent UV-resistance performance, compared to native cotton (Insufficient) and CQDs-cotton (Insufficient).

Via comparative overview with other researching reports in literature, cotton fabrics that were currently designed via doping of the as-prepared nanostructures are shown with significantly higher UV-resistance rather than that prepared in literature via the impregnation of metallic based ultraviolet/metallic absorber like Ag, Au, Zn, Cu, or Ti [29,50,64], or via the immobilization of metal organic frameworks within fabric matrix [28,49]. Therefore, the represented currently results approved that, AgNPs-CQDs cotton could be identified as potential candidate for ultraviolet-protective textiles.

According to literature [65–70], the mechanism overview of antimicrobial affinity for the prepared nanostructures (CQDs & AgNPs doped-CQDs) could be cleared as follows; in aqueous medium, the prepared nanostructures could generate ROS (reactive oxygen species) i.e., oxygen and hydroxyl free radicals, that mainly acting in microbial cell death. The generated ROS were suggested to cohere then penetrate the cell wall of microbial cells for motivating the oxidative decomposition and fragmentation of nucleic acid (DNA & RNA), resulting in inhibition and corruption of the expression of genes. Moreover, ROS might act in inactivation of the intracellular proteins, corruption of peroxidation of lipids, dysfunction of mitochondria, gradual deterioration of the cell wall, and lastly followed by necrosis/apoptosis, and resulting in cell death.

In the current approach, the antimicrobial performance for the prepared fabrics was evaluated against three pathogens of gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus), gram negative bacteria (E. coli) and fungi (C. albicans) with inhibition zone technique. The demonstrated results in Table 3 obviously showed that, against the examined bacteria and fungi, AgNPs-CQDs@cotton showed the highest antimicrobial performance, while, the estimated value of inhibition zone diameter was 14 ± 1.5 mm, 18 ± 1.5 mm and 16 ± 1.5 mm, compared to CQDs@cotton that was analyzed to exhibit inhibition zone diameter of zero, against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans, respectively. Meaning that, excellence in the antimicrobial potency of AgNPs doped-CQDs was mainly correlated to the successive doping of AgNPs in addition to its highly controllable size and shape, for easier penetration through the cell wall of the tested microbial strains, leading to eventual cell death. in addition to [65,67,69,71], the superiority of AgNPs doped-CQDs as potential microbicide colloid could be attributed to their surface decoration with functional groups to liberate the reactive oxygen species (ROS), that were mainly responsible for the microbial cell death. So, in summarization, doping of AgNPs within CQDs sheets to be uploaded within cotton resulted in production of AgNPs-CQDs@cotton with excellent UV-resistance and antimicrobial performance.

Advancement in the textile industry and technology leading to increase the demand of the multi-functional textile products, in addition to the worldwide-scaled requirement of multi-efficacious textiles to be applicable in different purposes. Moreover, doping of inorganic metals within CQDs was reported to superiorly advance their functionality and optical activity. Meanwhile, numerous researches were interestingly considered with investigation of unique strategies for industrialization of efficacious textiles to find wide-scaled applicability in different purposes. Therefore, the demonstrated approach represents unique strategy for manufacturing of fluorescent/UV-resistant/microbicide cotton via exploitation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and silver nanoparticles-doped carbon quantum dots (AgNPs-CQDs) to be applicable as efficacious textiles. Whereas, both CQDs and AgNPs were formerly clustered from alginate biopolymer. Cotton fabrics were sequentially treated with CQDs, Ag-CQDs & AgNPs-CQDs to prepare CQDs@Cotton, Ag-CQDs@Cotton and AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton, respectively. Thermal stability, colorimetric properties/color strength, UV-resistance & antimicrobial potentiality were systematically studied for all the prepared samples. The obtained data revealed that AgNPs-CQDs@Cotton exhibited excellent UV-resistance and antimicrobial activity.
All authors agreed on their roles and the order of names in the manuscript and this on behalf of them.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Deanship of Scientific Research at Umm Al-Qura University for supporting this work by Grant Code: (22UQU4350527DSR17).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110249.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots as antibacterial photosensitizers and their polymer nanocomposite applications
